summaries.Apartment:
Apartment – KB Summary (internal)

Scope  
Emails about the residence itself: move-in/move-out logistics, access (keys, fobs, digital credentials), maintenance/repairs, damage claims, amenity issues, rent‐related adjustments, or platform-mediated bookings (e.g., Airbnb host reimbursements).

Sender intent / common requests  
• Tenants: report malfunctions, flooding, noise, or ask for key replacements and appliance help.  
• Property managers / platforms: seek confirmation of damages, reimbursement approval, or status updates on work orders.  
• Vendors: coordinate repair schedules or parts delivery.

Triage & response  
1. Safety/utility outages (water leaks, lockouts) – acknowledge within 1 hr, open high-priority ticket, notify on-call maintenance.  
2. Access or amenity issues – respond < 4 hrs with instructions or ETA for fix.  
3. Financial/claim items – verify photos, invoices, lease/Airbnb ID, then forward to Finance or PM for approval; reply to sender with expected timeline.  
Log every action in the building CRM; attach relevant docs.

Style & policy cues  
Use a calm, solution-oriented tone. No PII beyond unit # and first name. Cite policy clauses if denying reimbursement. Close with clear next steps and an SLA (“We’ll update you by…”).
summaries.Lydia Chilton:
Lydia-Chilton threads are almost entirely about logistics for ongoing research collaboration—confirming, canceling, or shifting meetings, quickly flagging calendar errors, and giving brief status updates on project progress (e.g., CHI deadline, tool demos). Typical asks include: “Can we push to Monday instead?”, “Any slot after 2:30 PM Tuesday?”, “I’ll be on Zoom; can you still meet today?” or notice of illness/travel that affects attendance.

Triage & response
1. Reply same-day; scheduling uncertainty blocks her work.  
2. State clear availability windows in EDT and propose at least two concrete alternatives.  
3. Immediately update or resend the Google Calendar invite; add Zoom link if remote.  
4. Acknowledge project milestones or constraints (conference deadlines, orientation, doctor’s appointment) and adjust expectations.

Style & policies
• Keep messages brief, friendly, and solution-oriented.  
• Use email subject line continuity (“Re: <original invite>”) so auto-threading works.  
• No PII or sensitive data beyond what’s needed for scheduling.  
• Confirm any changes explicitly to avoid double bookings.
summaries.Conference:
Conference  

Emails in this folder relate to academic or industry conferences (IUI, C&C, etc.). Typical senders include authors, reviewers, program-committee members, publication chairs, and grant applicants. Messages usually request deadline reminders, clarification of submission or review procedures (PCS/TAPS), troubleshooting help with uploads, or confirmation of travel-grant eligibility.

My handling priorities:  
1. Time-critical items (review-due reminders, “URGENT” upload issues) – reply or forward to the appropriate chair/support team within 12-24 h and confirm resolution.  
2. Grant or reimbursement queries – acknowledge receipt, confirm required documents, and flag for finance once complete.  
3. General confirmations (“I’ll submit by X date”) – a brief “Thanks, noted” suffices; no follow-up needed.

I log any new deadlines or action items in the shared conference tracker, CC co-chairs when decisions affect program quality or budget, and preserve the original thread for audit. Tone stays courteous, concise, and solution-oriented; avoid sharing reviewer identities or unpublished work outside authorized lists per ACM confidentiality policy.
summaries.Other:
Other (General & One-Off Correspondence) – Summary

What belongs here  
• Miscellaneous, low-stakes messages that don’t fit a specific workflow: quick acknowledgments (“Thanks, got it”), polite follow-ups, clarifications, brief scheduling or availability notes, informal networking introductions, rent/utility reminders, small payment or paperwork confirmations, etc.  
• Content is usually short, self-contained, and not tied to an ongoing project thread.

Sender intent & common asks  
• Confirm receipt, express thanks/apology, share a file/link, note a simple status change, or request a minor favor (e.g., resend doc, grant short extension, propose meeting times).  
• Rarely requires deep discussion; the ask is typically clear in 1–2 lines.

Recommended triage & response  
1. Scan for any explicit action item or deadline; handle immediately if ≤5 min.  
2. Reply with a concise acknowledgment or requested info; aim for <24 h turnaround.  
3. If action exceeds 5 min, create a lightweight task/reminder, then archive thread once resolved.  
4. Route elsewhere only if it unexpectedly involves legal, HR, or long-term project commitments.

Style & constraints  
Maintain a friendly, professional tone; mirror senders’ brevity; avoid sharing PII; CC only necessary parties.
summaries.UI Design:
UI Design folder = student traffic about the UI Design course’s graded work. Messages typically cover: • deadline extensions or late-pass usage for specific HWs • permission to resubmit or replace files on Gradescope/Canvas • requests for regrade windows or point clarifications • confirmation that an updated submission was received.  

Sender intent: secure leniency, ensure their work is counted, or clarify grading policy. They often ask for a new due date (“Saturday EOD?”), confirm allowed late-pass count, or verify that regrading is possible.  

Triage & response: 1. Check the course syllabus and current late-pass ledger before granting anything. 2. If compliant, approve and state the exact new deadline or resubmission procedure. 3. If outside policy, offer alternatives (partial credit, office hours). 4. Log any granted extension in the tracking sheet and notify co-TAs on Slack. Respond within 24 h, using an encouraging, concise tone (“No worries—submit by Sat 23:59 ET”).  

Policies: max two late passes/assignment, one regrade request within 7 days, all file uploads via Gradescope; never exchange files over email.
summaries.University:
University Category – Working Definition (approx. 150 words)

What fits here  
Messages from Columbia University students, faculty, or staff about academic administration, program milestones, funding, payroll, events, or general community announcements. Threads often reference course/degree requirements, office assignments, visa or payroll tickets, workshop attendance, graduation logistics, or departmental congratulations.

Sender intent & common asks  
• Clarify a procedural step (“Did my advisor submit the form?”)  
• Confirm receipt or completion (“I tapped my ID; can you update my attendance?”)  
• Notify schedule or health conflicts  
• Request reimbursement status or payroll corrections  
• Exchange brief congratulations on defenses or awards

Handling & response strategy  
1. Scan for time-sensitive actions (deadlines, missing documents, payroll cut-offs) and flag “Urgent.”  
2. If action is mine, reply within one business day with next steps and any required links/forms.  
3. If another office owns the task, forward with context and cc the requester.  
4. For purely congratulatory chains, no reply needed; archive.

Key guidelines  
Use formal yet warm tone, reference university policy numbers if cited, avoid sharing student PII outside the thread, and store final replies in the student’s advising folder.
summaries.Internships:
Internships – Knowledge-Base Summary (for Recruiting/HR)

Scope: All mail related to student or early-career internship programs—applications, inquiries, interview coordination, offer letters, onboarding paperwork, performance reviews, extension or return-offer discussions, and university partnership outreach.

Sender intent & common requests:  • Candidates: request information on open roles, eligibility, deadlines, visa or relocation support; submit résumé/portfolio; confirm interview logistics; ask about stipend, housing, or conversion to full-time.  • Managers/mentors: approve requisitions, share feedback, propose project scopes.  • Universities/organizations: seek program sponsorship, career-fair participation, or campus-talk speakers.

Triage & response: Acknowledge candidate emails within 2 business days. Forward qualified applications to Hiring Tracker and label “Intern-Pipeline.” Use recruiting templates for rejections, interview scheduling, and offer packages. Escalate visa/housing questions to Global Mobility. Time-stamp all decisions in ATS for audit compliance.

Policies & style: Follow equal-employment-opportunity, non-discrimination, and data-privacy rules; strip PII before sharing internally. Communications should be courteous, encouraging, and concise (≤200 words), with clear next steps and relevant links (application portal, FAQ).
summaries.Engineering:
Engineering emails revolve around the design, build, and maintenance of our products and infrastructure. Typical messages include bug or incident reports, feature or architecture proposals, code-review requests, release/status updates, vendor inquiries about hardware / software, dependency or SLA notices, and cross-team alignment on technical roadmaps. Senders usually seek fast clarification, approval, or resourcing to keep development moving, or they escalate production issues requiring immediate mitigation.

On receipt, I triage by urgency and scope: P0 incidents route to the on-call channel within 15 minutes; P1–P3 items are logged in Jira with a reply-by expectation. Non-urgent design docs get acknowledged within one business day and assigned reviewers. For bug reports, I attempt minimal repro, capture logs, open/attach a ticket, and confirm next steps. Replies stay concise, action-oriented, and reference ticket numbers, doc links, or meeting slots.

Policies: never email source code or secrets; use internal vocabulary, RFC-style numbering, and markdown snippets; CC relevant leads, omit PII, and label external threads “CONFIDENTIAL.”
summaries.Appa:
Appa (placeholder category – no sample messages provided)

Until real examples are supplied, treat “Appa” as an empty holding bin rather than an active queue.

What belongs here  
• No definitive themes, senders, or requests are yet established.  
• Park any message whose relevance is unclear and does not fit existing categories, so it can be reviewed later without disrupting normal flow.

Likely sender intent  
• Unknown. Assume low-urgency until proven otherwise.  
• Could range from internal testing to future projects.

Handling and triage  
1. Move genuine mis-filed mail to its correct category as soon as intent is apparent.  
2. If the content is ambiguous, acknowledge receipt with a brief, neutral reply (“Thanks, I’m routing this to the right team and will follow up”).  
3. Flag items for weekly review with the ops lead to refine category rules.

Response strategy  
• Keep replies concise, request clarification when needed, and avoid committing to actions before the email’s purpose is confirmed.

Key notes  
• Do not delete or archive unseen.  
• Once real traffic patterns emerge, update this summary and rename the folder if appropriate.
summaries.Kavya Sreedhar:
This folder collects all correspondence from or specifically regarding Kavya Sreedhar—e-mails she sends directly, messages that CC/BCC her, or notes about tasks, meetings, or documents tied to her work. Typical intents include requesting status updates, sharing project deliverables, scheduling or rescheduling meetings, providing feedback, or escalating issues that require quick alignment.  

Triage guidance:  
• Flag as “Kavya” on arrival and skim immediately for time-sensitive asks (deadlines, meeting times, approval requests).  
• If an action or reply is needed within 24 hours, mark High Priority and draft a response the same business day; otherwise, slot into your next daily review.  
• For coordination items, loop in relevant stakeholders in a single, concise thread to avoid fragmentation.  

Response style: professional yet collaborative—acknowledge her points, confirm next steps in bullet form, and, when forwarding, include a 1-sentence summary for context. Maintain confidentiality; do not share her messages outside the project team without explicit permission.
summaries.Kavya:
Category “Kavya” currently has no sample messages, so the description below is provisional. Update it once real emails are available.

What belongs here  
• Any thread where the primary sender or recipient named “Kavya” is the focal point—e.g., project updates, questions, approvals, or personal check-ins directed to or from Kavya.  
• Topics may range from day-to-day task coordination and status reports to scheduling, resource requests, or feedback solicitation.

Typical sender intent  
• Share progress, ask for decisions/clarifications, or provide deliverables.  
• Request meetings, documents, or quick confirmations.

Handling & triage  
1. Scan for actionable items (deadlines, approvals, blockers).  
2. If action or response is needed within 24 h, flag “High Priority”; otherwise archive with the project label.  
3. Route specialized queries (e.g., budget, legal) to the appropriate internal owner and CC Kavya.  
4. Summarize long threads for Kavya if multiple stakeholders reply.

Response strategy  
• Acknowledge receipt quickly, outline next steps, and give realistic timelines.  
• Keep replies concise, bullet key decisions, and highlight any dependencies Kavya must address.

Policies & style  
• Use first-person, professional tone.  
• Preserve quoted context; avoid unnecessary CCs unless escalation is required.
summaries.Tao Long:
Tao Long – Summary  

Scope  
• Messages sent by, on behalf of, or directly concerning an individual or entity named “Tao Long.”  
• Likely covers project updates, task assignments, clarifications, approvals, or scheduling related to ongoing joint work.  

Typical sender intent  
• Provide status reports, share deliverables, request feedback or decisions, flag blockers, or arrange meetings.  
• May also include invoices, contract documents, or technical questions that require subject-matter input.  

Triage & response  
1. Scan for due dates, action items, and attached files; tag with priority (high = deadline ≤3 days).  
2. Acknowledge receipt within one business day, even if a full answer will follow later.  
3. Route specialized queries to the appropriate team lead; copy Tao Long only on need-to-know threads to minimize clutter.  
4. File finalised items in project folder; archive informational notes after logging key points.  

Style & policies  
• Keep replies concise, bullet-pointed where possible, and reference specific document versions.  
• Use courteous, professional tone; avoid jargon unless previously shared.
summaries.Receipts:
Receipts covers any email that serves as proof of payment or billing record. Typical examples include order confirmations, itemized invoices, subscription renewals, donation acknowledgments, travel or meal reimbursements, refunds, tax-deductible receipts, and monthly statements from utilities or SaaS vendors. The sender’s intent is usually to document a completed or pending transaction, provide payment details, or request settlement.

Triage: 
1. Verify legitimacy (sender domain, purchase amount, known vendor).  
2. Rename/label with date-vendor-amount for easy search.  
3. File to the Receipts folder or forward to accounting/expense tool within 24 h.  
4. Flag discrepancies (unknown charge, incorrect amount, missing VAT/GST details) and escalate to finance or the vendor.  
5. Keep in accordance with retention policy (minimum 7 yrs for tax documentation unless superseded).

Response strategy: No reply needed unless clarification, refund, or dispute is required. If replying, be concise, reference order/invoice number, and avoid sharing unnecessary personal or payment data.

Always protect PII and payment details, ensure attachments are virus-scanned, and never click links from unverified sources.
summaries.Accounts:
Accounts – Knowledge-Base Summary (for internal use)

What belongs here  
I file any message that concerns customer or staff user accounts: sign-up or sign-in problems, password resets, two-factor setup, billing-profile changes, account ownership transfers, duplicate or locked accounts, email-address updates, profile data corrections, or requests to close/reactivate an account.

Typical sender intent  
Senders usually need immediate access restored, credentials updated, or personal/billing details changed. Some seek confirmation that sensitive data has been removed or that security settings are in place. Others report suspicious activity and request an investigation.

Handling & response strategy  
1. Verify identity first (registered email, last four digits of card on file, or security questions).  
2. Check account status in the admin console and review recent activity logs.  
3. Apply the requested change or escalate to Security/Billing if elevated privileges are required.  
4. Confirm actions taken, outline next steps, and remind users of self-service options (password reset link, account dashboard). Aim for same-day resolution; flag unresolved tickets after 24 h.

Policies & style  
Adhere to GDPR/CCPA data-handling rules, never email full credentials, and redact payment details beyond last four digits. Use clear, empathetic language and end with a security tip when relevant.
summaries.Lydia:
Category Overview  
“Lydia” captures all correspondence in which the primary sender is Lydia [Last Name] (or messages clearly forwarded on her behalf). In the absence of topical constraints, expect a mix of personal check-ins, project updates, scheduling requests, and occasional document shares.

Typical Sender Intent  
• Share status updates or next steps on joint work  
• Ask for feedback, decisions, or quick approvals  
• Propose or adjust meeting times  
• Send reference materials, links, or attachments

Triage & Handling  
1. Scan for actionable items first (deadlines, questions, meeting invites).  
2. If a response or deliverable is needed within 48 hours, flag “High Priority.”  
3. File informational or FYI-only messages as “Archive—Lydia” after skim-reading.  
4. Add any meeting requests to the calendar immediately and confirm availability.  
5. Log decisions or task assignments in the relevant project tracker.

Response Guidelines  
• Aim to reply within one business day; acknowledge even when a full answer will follow later.  
• Use a friendly, collaborative tone; mirror Lydia’s level of detail.  
• Confirm receipt of attachments, clarify ambiguous requests, and outline next steps.
summaries.Columbia Business School Behavioral Rese:
Columbia Business School Behavioral Research Lab emails revolve around the day-to-day operation of CBS’s human-subjects research facility. Typical senders include faculty, PhD students, research assistants, and potential participants. Messages generally fall into four buckets: (1) study logistics—booking lab rooms, reserving time slots, requesting equipment or Qualtrics licenses; (2) compliance—IRB approvals, consent-form revisions, data-security questions; (3) participant management—recruitment blasts, no-show follow-ups, incentive‐payment issues; and (4) staffing—RA hiring, training schedules, and shift swaps.

Intent is usually to secure resources, confirm procedural details, or resolve operational hiccups quickly so studies stay on timeline. First triage step: verify whether the request is internal (CBS affiliate) or external; internal requests take priority. Next, confirm study has current IRB clearance before approving any lab use or participant outreach. Routine reservations can be acknowledged with a brief confirmation; policy or compliance questions should be escalated to the Lab Manager or the Associate Director of Research Compliance within one business day.

Style: concise replies, bullet points for action items, always reference study title and IRB #. Never share participant PII over email; direct payment inquiries to the secure Participant Payment portal.
summaries.Matthew J. Connelly:
This folder captures direct or strongly related traffic involving Matthew J. Connelly—e-mails he sends, messages addressed to him, or threads where he is the primary subject. Typical content includes:  • project or manuscript updates, data or document requests, and research feedback; • scheduling of meetings, lectures, or media interviews; • permissions to cite or reproduce material; • logistical questions (travel, honoraria, av support); • informal check-ins or networking outreach. Senders generally seek a decision, deliverable, or confirmation within a defined timeframe.  

Triage steps  
1. Scan for time-sensitive asks (deadlines, event dates) and flag “High Priority.”  
2. File reference material (attachments, manuscripts) in the shared drive; acknowledge receipt.  
3. Draft concise replies that confirm next actions, responsibilities, and due dates; cc relevant collaborators.  
4. If legal, contractual, or press-related, loop in the admin or communications lead.  

Policies/Style  
• Uphold confidentiality of unpublished research.  
• Use a courteous, decisive tone; limit threads to one clear subject.  
• Archive closed threads weekly to keep the folder actionable.
summaries.Inter:
Inter  

This folder is reserved for internal-only messages exchanged between colleagues, managers, and cross-functional teams. Mail here typically includes project status updates, meeting invitations, hand-offs, quick how-to questions, approvals, resource or access requests, and short decision threads. Senders usually want timely alignment, clarification, or confirmation rather than lengthy discussion.  

Handling strategy:  
• Scan new items at least twice daily, flag anything blocking deliverables, and reply or delegate within 4 business hours.  
• When a decision or action is required, confirm scope, deadline, and responsible owners in the first reply; copy relevant stakeholders and link to supporting docs.  
• Archive threads once closed, but tag those containing key decisions with “Record” for easy retrieval.  

Policies & style:  
Internal information is confidential—never forward outside the organization without approval. Use concise bullets, hyperlinks instead of attachments when possible, and include clear subject prefixes (e.g., “ACTION:”, “INFO:”, “FYI:”). Follow house style for tone: professional, direct, and solution-oriented.
summaries.Univiersity:
University Category – Triage Summary (150 words)

What belongs here  
• Any message originating from a university-affiliated domain (.edu or official sub-domain) or clearly referencing campus units (admissions, registrar, bursar, academic departments, research offices, alumni relations, student services).  
• Content ranges from application/acceptance packets, enrollment & course-registration notices, tuition or financial-aid statements, internship/co-op paperwork, faculty or advisor outreach, event and workshop invitations, research collaboration requests, and alumni or development campaigns.

Typical sender intent & requests  
• Inform about deadlines, policy changes, account balances, or event logistics.  
• Request documents, confirmations, signatures, meeting times, or payment.  
• Solicit participation in surveys, studies, or fundraising.

Recommended handling  
1. Scan for hard deadlines and flag them immediately.  
2. If action is required (forms, payments, RSVPs), create a task and acknowledge within one business day.  
3. Forward niche academic or financial queries to the respective campus contact while CC’ing the original sender.  
4. Archive completed threads in “University” for future reference.

Key policies & style  
• Observe FERPA/privacy rules—never include personal identifiers in replies.  
• Use formal salutations, concise bullet points, and attach files as locked PDFs when possible.
summaries.Matt J. Connelly:
Matt J. Connelly – Category Summary (120–180 words)

Scope & Themes:
Emails arrive directly from Matt J. Connelly’s personal or company address. Content ranges from strategic updates, approvals, feedback on ongoing projects, scheduling requests, and occasional quick questions. Messages are usually conversational but action-oriented, often containing attachments, links, or directives that affect current priorities.

Sender Intent & Common Requests:
• Provide guidance or decisions requiring acknowledgement.
• Request status reports, metrics, or draft materials.
• Propose or confirm meetings, travel, or deliverable timelines.
• Share documents for review or approval.

Handling & Triage:
1. Flag as “High Priority” and read immediately; Matt’s notes often drive next steps for multiple teams.
2. Extract clear action items—capture in task tracker within 15 minutes.
3. Acknowledge receipt the same business day, even if a full answer needs more time.
4. If unclear, ask concise follow-up questions rather than assume.

Response Style:
Use a direct, professional tone. Open with a brief acknowledgment, list bullet-pointed responses or decisions, and confirm deadlines. Avoid unnecessary CCs unless cross-functional input is required.
summaries.Medical:
Medical Category – Knowledge-Base Summary (150 words)

Scope  
Includes any email that mentions patient care, health records, appointments, test or imaging results, prescriptions, insurance pre-authorizations, medical billing, or clinical research. Typical senders are physicians, nurses, clinics, laboratories, pharmacies, insurers, and patients/caregivers.

Typical intent  
• Schedule, reschedule, or cancel appointments  
• Request or transmit lab/imaging results and clinical notes  
• Seek prescription renewals or medication guidance  
• Clarify billing codes, copays, or claim status  
• Provide pre-procedure instructions or follow-up plans  
• Submit consent forms or referral documents

Triage & response  
1. Flag urgent/acute-care items and route to on-call clinician within 1 hour.  
2. For routine matters, acknowledge within 24 hours and assign to the appropriate specialty team.  
3. Use secure messaging or encrypted attachment when PHI is present.  
4. Log all actions in the patient’s EMR for audit continuity.

Policies & style  
Comply with HIPAA and local privacy laws. Never include diagnosis or sensitive results in the subject line. Use the approved clinical email template, closing with the standard confidentiality disclaimer.
summaries.Sitong Wang:
Sitong Wang-tagged email threads originate from a single high-priority stakeholder—likely a project collaborator, supervisor, or VIP client—whose notes, questions, or directives require quick turnaround. Messages typically include one or more of the following: (1) status checks on shared deliverables, (2) requests for data, files, or code snippets, (3) feedback on drafts, designs, or analyses, (4) scheduling or agenda confirmation for meetings, or (5) approvals and sign-offs. The sender’s intent is almost always action-oriented: clarify next steps, obtain specific information, or unblock progress.

Triage guidance  
• Flag as “Important” and aim to acknowledge within 4 business hours; provide substantive reply within 24.  
• Scan for deadlines or explicit asks; summarize them at the top of your response.  
• If additional input is needed, loop in the relevant teammate immediately and copy Sitong.  
• Store attachments in the shared project drive, then give Sitong share-links rather than re-sending large files.

Style & policy  
Respond in a concise, professional tone; use numbered lists for requested actions; close with a clear statement of what you will do next and by when.
summaries.Disposables:
Disposables emails cover anything related to single-use or consumable items our organization orders, stocks, or distributes—e.g., gloves, masks, cups, printer cartridges, sanitary supplies, or other expendables. Senders are usually internal staff flagging low inventory, facilities or lab teams requesting urgent restocks, or external vendors forwarding quotes, shipment notices, invoices, or quality alerts. Common asks: “Please reorder 10 cases,” “PO attached,” “ETA on back-ordered gloves?” or “Batch X found defective—advise.” 

Triage quickly: 
1. Verify quantity/sku and needed-by date; compare against current stock in the inventory system.  
2. If under reorder threshold, forward to Purchasing with required specs; copy requester.  
3. For vendor emails, log delivery or invoice in the procurement queue and reconcile against the PO.  
4. Escalate quality or safety concerns to Facilities or QA within 2 hours, marking email HIGH. 

Respond briefly: acknowledge within same business day, give next steps or ETA, and close loop once fulfilled. Follow our Sustainable Procurement Policy—prioritize eco-friendly alternatives and confirm disposal compliance where relevant.
